rulings.Case Title: NINA PINZARRONE VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA - A SOVEREIGN STATE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 4: DEFENDANT FEDERAL EXPRESS CORP.'S, A DELAWARE CORPORATION, MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Motion for Summary Judgment is granted. "There is a triable issue of material fact if, and only if, the evidence would allow a reasonable trier of fact to find the underlying fact in favor of the party opposing the motion in accordance with the applicable standard of proof." [See Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 C4th 826, 850.] To prevail in negligence, Plaintiff must show that Defendants owed a legal duty, that Defendants breached that duty, and that the breach proximately caused their injuries. [See Wiener v. Southcoast Childcare Centers, Inc., (2004) 32 Cal. 4th 1138, 1145.] When the pleaded facts do not naturally give rise to an inference of causation, plaintiff must plead specific facts "explaining how the conduct caused or contributed to the injury." Bockrath v. Aldrich Chem. Co., Inc. (1999) 21 C4th 71, 78. Under Saelzler v. Advanced Group 400 (2001) 25 Cal.4th 763, the court provided guidelines when determining whether causation exists. (1) proof cannot be based on speculation or conjecture; (2) Plaintiff must demonstrate substantial link between Defendant's conduct and injury; (3) Plaintiff must show it is more likely than not that Defendant's alleged breach was cause of injury; (4) cause cannot be inferred from hindsight; (5) the burden of proof on causation cannot be shifted to the defendant. Here, Defendant has met its initial burden by showing the essential element of causation to Plaintiff's negligence claim has not been established. Plaintiff does not raise a triable issue of material fact in opposition. Plaintiff alleges that Federal Express caused damage to the sidewalk, and this damage caused Plaintiff's fall and injuries. However, Plaintiff has not plead with specific facts which trucks caused the damage, when the damage occurred, or any admissible evidence showing Plaintiff's injury was caused by damage to the sidewalk. Plaintiff presents no evidence of a substantial link between Federal Express and Plaintiff's injuries. Plaintiff has not shown it was more likely than not that Federal Express caused Plaintiff's injuries. Thus, because Plaintiff has not established a prima facie case of causation, Defendant is entitled to summary judgment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: NINA PINZARRONE VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA - A SOVEREIGN STATE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 4: DEFENDANT UNITED PARCEL SERVICE, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Motion for Summary Judgment is granted. "There is a triable issue of material fact if, and only if, the evidence would allow a reasonable trier of fact to find the underlying fact in favor of the party opposing the motion in accordance with the applicable standard of proof." [See Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 C4th 826, 850.] To prevail in negligence, Plaintiff must show that Defendants owed a legal duty, that Defendants breached that duty, and that the breach proximately caused their injuries. [See Wiener v. Southcoast Childcare Centers, Inc., (2004) 32 Cal. 4th 1138, 1145.] When the pleaded facts do not naturally give rise to an inference of causation, plaintiff must plead specific facts "explaining how the conduct caused or contributed to the injury." Bockrath v. Aldrich Chem. Co., Inc. (1999) 21 C4th 71, 78. Under Saelzler v. Advanced Group 400 (2001) 25 Cal.4th 763, the court provided guidelines when determining whether causation exists. (1) proof cannot be based on speculation or conjecture; (2) Plaintiff must demonstrate substantial link between Defendant's conduct and injury; (3) Plaintiff must show it is more likely than not that Defendant's alleged breach was cause of injury; (4) cause cannot be inferred from hindsight; (5) the burden of proof on causation cannot be shifted to the defendant. Here, Defendant has met its initial burden by showing the essential element of causation to Plaintiff's negligence claim has not been established. Plaintiff does not raise a triable issue of material fact in opposition. Plaintiff alleges that UPS caused damage to the sidewalk, and this damage caused Plaintiff's fall and injuries. However, Plaintiff has not plead with specific facts which trucks caused the damage, when the damage occurred, or any admissible evidence showing Plaintiff's injury was caused by damage to the sidewalk. Plaintiff presents no evidence of a substantial link between UPS and Plaintiff's injuries. Plaintiff has not shown it was more likely than not that UPS caused Plaintiff's injuries. Thus, because Plaintiff has not established a prima facie case of causation, Defendant is entitled to summary judgment. rulings.Case Title: LINDSEY E. SCULLY VS. MENLO CAPITAL GROUP, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendant St. Louis Real Estate Advisors Llc Dba Lee &Amp; Associates Of St Louis To The Following Discovery 1 Form Interrogatories 2 Special Interrogatories 3 Requests For Production Of Documents And 4 Requests For Admission
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFF LINDSEY SCULLY'S Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendant St. Louis Real Estate Advisors Llc Dba Lee &Amp; Associates Of St Louis To The Following Discovery 1 Form Interrogatories 2 Special Interrogatories 3 Requests For Production Of Documents And 4 Requests For Admission. Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: This motion is continued to September 10, 2015 at 10 a.m., to give Defendants St. Louis Real Estate Advisors LLC, dba Lee & Associates of St Louis ("St. Louis") the opportunity to comply with Local Rule 2.6B. St. Louis must deliver courtesy copies of the opposition papers to Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins at WOOD ROBBINS LLP, 1 Post Street, Suite 800, San Francisco, CA 94104, with a cover letter stating the new hearing date, no later than August 3, 2015 at 4:00 pm or the Motion will be GRANTED. Any exhibit must be separated by an exhibit tab per California Rules of Court 3.1110(f). The court has courtesy copies of the moving papers. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: LINDSEY E. SCULLY VS. MENLO CAPITAL GROUP, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of The Menlo Defendants Motion For Sanctions Against Plaintiff Lindsey Scully
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 1, DEFENDANT MENLO CAPITAL GROUP, LLC ET AL, MENLO REALTY INCOME PROPERTIES 27, LLC, MENLO REALTY INCOME PROPERTIES 28, LLC, SUNIL SURI, REALTY INCOME CORPORATION, MENLO NET LEASE HOLDINGS, LLC'S Motion For Sanctions Against Plaintiff Lindsey Scully. Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants' Sunil Suri, Menlo Capital Group, LLC, Menlo Net Lease Holdings, LLC, Realty Income Corporation, Menlo Realty Income Properties 27, LLC, and Menlo Realty Income Properties 28 LLC (collectively "Menlo Defendants") Motion for Sanctions is DENIED. Plaintiff Lindsey E. Scully ("Scully") shall prepare a form of the order recapitulating this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. Menlo Defendants seek sanctions under the authority of the Code of Civil Procedure as the prevailing party in a prior discovery motion. Menlo Defendants argue that Scully's failed Motions to Compel of April 2015 were not made with "substantial justification" and thus sanctions must issue. See Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2030.300(d). But Menlo Defendants failed to seek sanctions in opposing the April 2015 Motions, and thus sanctions have been waived. There may indeed be a mechanism by which a party can resurrect a request for sanctions after having failed to seek them during earlier process. But that mechanism would involve bringing an additional motion for reconsideration. Scully correctly argues that such a motion for reconsideration must be brought within ten days. In this case it was not brought at all. And although the Court has authority to sua sponte bring its own motion for reconsideration, it declines to do so. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: LINDSEY E. SCULLY VS. MENLO CAPITAL GROUP, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Menlo Defendants Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set Two And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFF LINDSEY SCULLY'S Motion To Compel Menlo Defendants Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set Two And Request For Sanctions (part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Lindsey E. Scully's ("Scully") Motion to Compel Further Responses is DENIED. Having declined to request monetary sanctions, having failed to note the request for sanctions on the caption page, having failed to support any request for sanctions with facts in declaration, and having failed to argue in any substantive way for sanctions in memorandum, the prevailing Parties, Defendants Sunil Suri, Menlo Capital Group, LLC, Menlo Net Lease Holdings, LLC, Realty Income Corporation, Menlo Realty Income Properties 27, LLC, and Menlo Realty Income Properties 28 LLC (collectively "Menlo Defendants") SHALL RECOVER NO SANCTIONS, and sanctions as against all parties are DENIED. As the prevailing party, Menlo Defendants shall prepare a form of the order recapitulating this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. After serving multiple rounds of discovery, Scully finally served Form Interrogatory No. 17.1-for the first time-on Menlo Defendants in mid-April 2015. Menlo Defendants argue that the 45 day rule vitiates this Court's jurisdiction to hear the Motion. But they are mistaken. Menlo Defendants responded to Form Interrogatory 17.1 via mail on May 14, 2015. Forty-five days runs on June 28, 2015, a Sunday, making responses due the following Monday, June 29, 2015. And 5 days are added for mail service. This puts any notice of motion to be served on July 6, 2015, the very day the Instant Motion was, in fact, filed and served. More than 45 days have not run. Menlo Defendants argue that the RFAs that predicate Form Interrogatory 17.1 were themselves served many months prior, effectively triggering the 45 day rule. But Scully did not seek additional responses to RFAs and does not move to compel further responses to RFAs (at least not against Menlo Defendants). The fact that the RFAs provide a factual reference point upon which additional interrogatories are based does not affect the 45 day rule with regard to newly issued interrogatories. (see part 2) =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: LINDSEY E. SCULLY VS. MENLO CAPITAL GROUP, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Lindsey Scully Notice Of Motion To Compel Menlo Defendants Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set Two And Request For Sanctions (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 1, Plaintiff Lindsey Scully'S Motion To Compel Menlo Defendants Further Responses To Form Interrogatories, Set Two And Request For Sanctions (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling Only) (part 2of 2). But Menlo Defendants' second argument works where their 45-day rule argument fails. A plain textual reading of the Judicial Council's Form Interrogatory 17.1 compels this Court to conclude that the language does indeed, contemplate the contemporaneous service of fresh RFAs with the Form Interrogatory. This is not to say, and the Court does not rule, that Scully cannot ask, either in written discovery, or in oral depositions, as to the Menlo Defendants' factual contentions that support their denials (or admissions for that matter) to RFAs. But the Court does hold that Scully cannot invoke Form Interrogatory 17.1 as a way to acquire information related to previously issued RFAs-distinct from concomitantly served RFAs. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)

rulings.Case Title: WILDA L WHITE VS. GIRISH SUBRAMANYAN MD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Girish Subramanyan Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Written Discovery And Request For Sanctions (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 2, DEFENDANT GIRISH SUBRAMANYAN Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Written Discovery And Request For Sanctions (Part 2 of 3) (Continued from Part 1 of 3) White argues that Defendants did not make a serious effort to meet and confer. But Defendants sent a letter via e-mail and U.S. Mail on April 17, 2015, to which White, apparently has never responded. White states in declaration that she did not receive an "e-mail" meet and confer on this issue. But Defendants also sent the missive via U.S. Mail. Conspicuously, White fails to state under penalty of perjury that she did not receive a "letter." In any event, certainly now that the Instant Motion has been filed, White is well aware of the issue, and even now, White still refuses to amend her responses. This suggests that even in the context of a more robust meet and confer, White's discovery responses would have been unchanged. REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION In response to Requests for Production Nos. 1-2, White refused to produce any documents of any kind, objecting under the protection of a then-pending motion to quash. But this motion to quash was actually a combined Motion to Quash Deposition Subpoenas and a Motion for Compliance. See Order Re Motion to Quash Deposition Subpoenas, July 2, 2015. It did not relate to requests for production of documents served on party. It did not have anything to do with the Request for Production served on White. Thus, White's objections to RFPD 1-2 were inapt and are OVERRULED. Finally Defendants seek full responses to RFPD No. 11. Defendants argue in Separate Statement that White's response was inadequate because it only offered evidence of receipts and the like for six of the 163 itemized expenses. To this White has no response. White's failure to file a Separate Statement is fatal. See Cal. R. Ct. Rule 3.1345; Mills v. U.S. Bank, 166 Cal. App. 4th 871, 893 (2008) ("We conclude that because Plaintiffs did not comply with the requirements of former rule 335 [now Rule 3.1345], the trial court was well within its discretion to deny the motion to compel discovery on that basis."). But the Court declines to strike White's opposition on this basis and instead examines White's argument in briefing. (Continued Part 3 of 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: WILDA L WHITE VS. GIRISH SUBRAMANYAN MD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Girish Subramanyan Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Written Discovery And Request For Sanctions (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 2, DEFENDANT GIRISH SUBRAMANYAN Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Written Discovery And Request For Sanctions (Part 3 of 3) (Continued from Part 2 of 3) In briefing, White argues that she has in fact "provided defendant all the documents that were responsive to his request and were within her possession and control at the time she answered the discovery request." This may be true, but it does not obviate White's obligation to say-so in a verified code-compliant response. Here she has not done so. Certainly the Court cannot and does not order White to produce documents she does not have. But White still must make verified code compliant responses under Cal. Civ. Proc. Code §§ 2031.210 - 2031.320. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: WILDA L WHITE VS. GIRISH SUBRAMANYAN MD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Written Discovery And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 2, DEFENDANT GIRISH SUBRAMANYAN Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Written Discovery And Request For Sanctions (Part 1 of 3) Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants' Girish Subramanyan, M.D., and San Francisco Psychiatrists, Inc. (collectively "Defendants'") Motion for to Compel Further Responses to Written Discovery is GRANTED. Having failed to request sanctions in the notice of motion, and having failed to note the request for sanctions on the caption page, Defendants' request for sanctions is DENIED. As the prevailing party Defendants shall prepare a form of the order recapitulating this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES The Discovery Act mandates response to interrogatories in one of the following three ways: either by "an answer containing the information sought," by an "exercise of the party's option to produce writings," or by an "objection to the particular interrogatory." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2030.210(a). Here, in Special Interrogatories Nos. 16-19. Defendants inquired as to Plaintiff Wilda White's ("White's") economic damages. White responded with one objection and one objection only: that she did not understand the term "economic damages." But the phrase "economic damages" is not particularly mysterious. And if White did not understand it, she had an obligation to retain a lawyer who could explain it to her. Moreover, even White concedes in briefing that the phrase is defined in the Code. See Cal. Civ. Code § 1431.2(b)(1) (explaining that "the term 'economic damages' means objectively verifiable monetary losses including medical expenses, loss of earnings, burial costs, loss of use of property, costs of repair or replacement, costs of obtaining substitute domestic services, loss of employment and loss of business or employment opportunities"). Why White did not answer the Interrogatories based on this definition is not explained. (Continued in Part 2 of 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KAYVAN KHATAMI VS. ENNOV SOLUTIONS, INC., A LIMITED LIABILITY PARTNER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 8: DEFENDANT ENNOV SOLUTIONS, INC.'S, A LIMITED LIABILITY PARTNERSHIP, DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. The demurrer to the intentional infliction of emotional distress cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. See CCP 430.10(a). The worker's compensation exclusivity provisions bar the cause of action. The court can address the issue on demurrer. In Gibbs v. American Airlines, Inc. (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 1, 13, the court explains: Singleton affirmed judgment following an employer's demurrer where the plaintiff alleged injury to an employee within the course of employment but made no mention of workers' compensation insurance. [citation] Importantly, the Court of Appeal found it proper to presume that the employer complied with the law requiring its purchase of workers' compensation insurance. [citation] A plaintiff's action against an employer upon allegations of a work-related injury is barred unless the plaintiff also alleges that the employer has failed to secure the payment of workers' compensation through mandated insurance. Here, pars. 10-12 of the complaint allege the employment relationship and there is no allegation that defendant lacked the requisite worker's compensation insurance. The injury occurred during plaintiff's employment. (Pars. 10-12 of the Amended Complaint). The court further notes that plaintiff fails to state a cause of action because the failure to pay wages is not outrageous conduct as a matter of law. The court notes that defendant did not raise CCP 430.10(e) [failure to state a cause of action] or argue the outrageousness issue in its moving papers. Both parties, however, briefed the issue in the opposition and reply briefs. Plaintiff cites Cramer v. Consolidated Freightways, Inc. 255 F.3d 683, 697 (9th Cir. 2001) for the proposition that defendant's "criminal" conduct is outrageous per se. Cramer is distinguishable. There, the defendant concealed cameras in restrooms and violated Penal Code sec. 653n. In this simple wage dispute, plaintiff is merely alleging civil violations of the Labor Code. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: KAYVAN KHATAMI VS. ENNOV SOLUTIONS, INC., A LIMITED LIABILITY PARTNER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 8: DEFENDANT ENNOV SOLUTIONS, INC.'S, A LIMITED LIABILITY PARTNERSHIP, MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. The motion is denied as moot as to pars. 14-19 [IIED] in light of the ruling on the demurrer. The motion is granted without leave to amend as to Par. (b) of the prayer [general/special damages] and Par. d [punitive damages]. Plaintiff cannot obtain those damages because they are only recoverable on the IIED cause of action. The motion is denied as to Par. (c) of the prayer [front and back pay]. Plaintiff can recover back pay based on the overtime. The motion is granted without leave to amend as to Par. (f) of the prayer [liquidated damages per Labor Code sec. 1194.2] as unopposed and plaintiff does not allege that he was not paid the minimum wage. The motion is denied as to Par. (g) of the prayer [statutory/civil penalties]. Labor Code secs. 203 and 226 support the prayer. The motion is denied as to Par. (h) of the prayer [injunction]. Plaintiff can recover that relief under the UCL and Labor Code sec.226(h) The motion is denied as to Par. (j) of the prayer [attorney's fees]. Labor Code sec. 218.5 supports the award. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: GIOVANNIE MONTOYA VS. YELLOW CAB COOPERATIVE, INC., A CORPORATION, DBA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendant Sogolon Djata To Provide Responses To Plaintiff Montoya Form Interrogatories - General, Set One, Special Interrogatories, Set One, And Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 3, 2015, Line 3, PLAINTIFF GIOVANNIE MONTOYA'S Motion To Compel Defendant Sogolon Djata To Provide Responses To Plaintiff Montoya Form Interrogatories - General, Set One, Special Interrogatories, Set One, And Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One And Request For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: This Motion is continued to September 10, 2015 at 10 a.m., to give Plaintiff Giovannie Montoya ("Montoya") the opportunity to comply with Local Rule 2.6B. Montoya must deliver courtesy copies of the motion papers to Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins at WOOD ROBBINS LLP, 1 Post Street, Suite 800, San Francisco, CA 94104, with a cover letter stating the new hearing date, no later than August 3, 2015 at 4:00 pm or the Motion will be DENIED. Any exhibit must be separated by an exhibit tab per California Rules of Court 3.1110(f). Montoya is further ordered to serve this order on all other parties to this action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EG)
